beats.narration_text: Hej — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to create a slash command, add a command, write a custom command, or get guidance on slash command structure — and the Command Development skill fires. Slash commands are Markdown files containing prompts that Claude executes during interactive sessions: define once, invoke with a slash, reuse across sessions. Let me walk you through the fundamental framing distinction, the three command locations, the five frontmatter fields, and the argument and file reference systems — then the teardown.

props.command: Create a slash command to review my code for security issues.
output: Markdown file · YAML frontmatter · directives TO Claude

beats.act: anatomy — for-Claude framing + three locations + five frontmatter fields
beats.narration_text: The most important framing in the skill: commands are instructions FOR Claude, not messages TO the user. When a user invokes slash-review, the command content becomes Claude's instructions. Write it as a directive — review this code for SQL injection, XSS, and authentication issues — not as a description — this command will review your code. The skill is explicit: always use the first approach. Commands live in three locations. Project commands in dot-claude-slash-commands are shared with the team and available in that project only — shown as (project) in slash-help. Personal commands in tilde-slash-dot-claude-slash-commands are available in all projects for the user's personal workflows — shown as (user). Plugin commands in plugin-name-slash-commands are available when the plugin is installed — shown as (plugin-name). The five YAML frontmatter fields are: description, shown in slash-help, best under 60 characters; allowed-tools, to restrict which tools the command can use, with patterns like Bash(git:*); model, to override the conversation model for this command — haiku for fast, sonnet for standard, opus for complex; argument-hint, to document expected arguments for autocomplete; and disable-model-invocation, to prevent programmatic invocation.

props.sparkLine: Location sets scope. allowed-tools sets blast radius.

beats.act: design — argument systems + file references + CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT
beats.narration_text: The command argument system has two modes. Dollar-ARGUMENTS captures all arguments as a single string — slash-fix-issue 123 expands dollar-ARGUMENTS to 123. Dollar-1, dollar-2, dollar-3 capture individual positional arguments — slash-review-pr 123 high alice gives dollar-1 as 123, dollar-2 as high, and dollar-3 as alice. Mix them: deploy dollar-1 to dollar-2 with options: dollar-3 captures the environment, version, and any remaining flags in one command. File references use the at-sign syntax. At-dollar-1 reads the file at the argument path before processing the command. Multiple at-signs read multiple files: at-src-slash-old.js and at-src-slash-new.js both get included. Static at-sign references embed known files — at-package.json, at-tsconfig.json — without arguments at all. Plugin commands get one extra capability: the CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT environment variable resolves to the plugin's absolute path. It makes plugin commands portable — no hardcoded paths, works across all installations. Reference plugin scripts, config files, and templates with dollar-{CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}-slash-scripts or dollar-{CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}-slash-config. The note in the skill: dot-claude-slash-commands is a legacy format — for new skills, prefer dot-claude-slash-skills-slash-name-slash-SKILL.md.

props.sparkLine: The body is Claude's directive. Write it that way.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The Command Development skill gets five things right. The for-Claude framing is the most teachable point and it is stated clearly with a correct and an incorrect example side by side — directive versus description. The three-location taxonomy maps cleanly to scope: project for team workflows, personal for cross-project utilities, plugin for bundled commands. Five frontmatter fields each have distinct purposes and explicit default values documented. Dollar-ARGUMENTS and dollar-1 positional systems are both covered with working examples. CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT solves the portability problem with a named, testable solution. Where it bites. Bash execution is the most-used dynamic command feature — the exclamation-mark backtick syntax — but it is deferred entirely to references-slash-plugin-features-reference.md with no inline example in the main skill. The legacy format note is buried in a Note block at the top rather than prominently flagged where new commands are discussed. The dollar-IF conditional pattern in argument validation examples is not a real template language — it is intended as Claude prose logic but could be misread as actual syntax. The Bash open-paren git-colon-star close-paren allowed-tools patterns are shown but the namespace scope rules are never explained. And plugin command auto-discovery from the commands directory is stated but not compared to the skills directory discovery to help users choose.

props.sparkLine: For-Claude framing solid. Bash execution deferred — that is the gap.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Command Development: Markdown files · YAML frontmatter · directives TO Claude, never messages TO user. Three locations: project (team) · personal (user-wide) · plugin (bundled). Five fields: description · allowed-tools · model · argument-hint · disable-model-invocation. Args: $ARGUMENTS (full) · $1/$2 (positional) · @ (file ref). Plugin: CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT for portable paths. Note: .claude/commands/ is legacy — prefer .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md. Gaps: bash syntax deferred · legacy buried · $IF not real syntax · Bash scope rules unexplained · commands vs skills discovery not compared.

artifactLines: Markdown files · YAML frontmatter · directives TO Claude, not user messages
5 fields: description · allowed-tools · model · argument-hint · disable-invoc
Gaps: bash deferred · legacy buried · $IF not real syntax · scope unexplained

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session and paste this: Create a slash command called review-pr that takes a PR number as an argument, reads the changed files, and reviews them for code quality. Watch five things. First: does the command body tell Claude what to do — review for quality, check style, provide feedback — or does it describe what will happen to the user? Second: does it use dollar-ARGUMENTS or dollar-1 for the PR number — and is the argument-hint set? Third: does it use the allowed-tools field to restrict to Bash(gh:*) and Read? Fourth: does it produce a flat or namespaced file layout — and why? Fifth: if you ask it to add bash execution to get the PR diff dynamically, does it show you the exclamation-mark backtick syntax inline, or does it send you to a reference file?

props.command: Create a slash command review-pr that takes a PR number, reads changed files, and reviews code quality.
props.runningText: Watch: for-Claude? $1 or $ARGUMENTS? allowed-tools? bash syntax inline?
output: Expected: directive body · argument-hint set · Bash(gh:*) allowed
Expected: bash ! syntax shown inline if dynamic context requested
Red flag: user-message body · no argument-hint · sends to reference file
